Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The Road Traffic Act Framework
The Road Traffic Acts span from 1961 to recent amending legislation. The framework has been substantially
modernised through periodic Acts (notably 1994, 2002, 2006, 2010, and subsequent). This chapter sets out the
architecture - the principal statutes, the categories of offences, the FCN system, and the Penalty Points system.

1.1 The Principal Statutes
Statute

Principal Content

Road Traffic Act 1961

Foundational statute; dangerous driving (s.51); careless driving (s.52); s.53
dangerous driving causing death/serious harm; driving licence offences (s.38);
insurance (s.56); failure to stop (s.106); stop power (s.108)

Road Traffic Act 1994

Modernised drink-driving framework (now largely superseded by RTA 2010)

Road Traffic Act 2002

Penalty Points system; FCN framework

Road Traffic Act 2006

MAT (Mandatory Alcohol Testing); FCN extensions; mobile phone offences

Road Traffic Act 2010

Modern drink-driving framework (s.4 driving while intoxicated; s.5 being in
charge); drug-driving framework; testing procedure (s.9-15); thresholds
reformed

Road Traffic (No.2) Act 2011

Further amendments

Road Traffic Act 2014

Lower BAC limits for learners / professional drivers; further enforcement

Road Traffic Act 2016

Drug-driving testing framework; further amendments

Road Traffic and Roads Act
2023

Recent amendments including scrambler offences

1.2 The FCN Framework
Fixed Charge Notice system created by RTA 2002 and extended by RTA 2006 / subsequent. Operational
features:
Specified offences (speeding, mobile phone, seatbelt, parking, certain other minor RTA offences).
Notice issued at scene or by post.
Driver pays within specified period (typically 28 days at lower rate; 28 further days at higher rate).
Non-payment results in summons.
Court conviction follows if defended unsuccessfully.
Penalty Points imposed in addition (where applicable).
FCN is administrative - no conviction unless court process engaged.

1.3 The Penalty Points System
Penalty Points system created by RTA 2002. Operational features:
Specified offences attract specified points (1-7 points depending on offence).
12 points within 3-year window results in 6-month disqualification.
Learner drivers - lower threshold (7 points).
Points administered automatically on FCN payment or court conviction.
Points display on driving licence record.
Specific RSA / Garda framework for management.

1.5 Garda Powers Map
s.108 RTA 1961 - power to stop a vehicle.
s.39 RTA 1961 - power to demand production of driving licence.
s.10 RTA 2010 - preliminary breath test.
s.12 RTA 2010 - station evidential breath test.
s.13 RTA 2010 - blood / urine sample at station.
s.14 RTA 2010 - refusal offences.
s.15 RTA 2010 - drug-driving testing.
s.6 RTA 2006 / s.10 RTA 2010 - MAT (Mandatory Alcohol Testing) checkpoint.
s.39 RTA 2002 - arrest power for drink-driving.

/

court

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Drink Driving - s.4 RTA 2010
s.4 RTA 2010
(The above is the s.5 'being in charge' framework. s.4 RTA 2010 covers driving / attempting to drive with
intoxicant present exceeding specified limit. The actual operational offence wording is set out in s.4 in detail.
The simplified framework: driving / attempting to drive in a public place with blood / urine / breath alcohol
concentration above specified limit.)

2.1 The Specified Limits
Category

BAC (blood)

Urine

Breath

General driver

50mg / 100ml

67mg / 100ml

22 micrograms /
100ml

20mg / 100ml

27mg / 100ml

9 micrograms
100ml

Specified driver
professional)

(learner,

novice,

/

'Specified driver' includes: learner permit holder; novice driver (within first 2 years); professional driver
(vehicle for hire / reward, public service, certain other categories). Lower limit reflects elevated risk standard.

2.2 Elements of the Offence
Driving or attempting to drive: Driving in fact; or making a serious attempt (key in ignition, vehicle started,
intention manifest). Mere presence in vehicle without attempt is not driving.
Mechanically propelled vehicle: Includes cars, vans, motorcycles, mopeds; also includes some non-traditional
vehicles (e-scooters subject to recent legislative framework).
Public place: Wide statutory definition - includes public roads, places of public resort, certain private places
to which the public have access.
Concentration of alcohol exceeding specified limit: Proven by evidential testing procedure (s.12 station
breath; s.13 blood/urine via MBRS analysis).
Within 3 hours: The evidential sample concentration is taken to reflect the time of driving / attempting where
within 3-hour window (the 'within 3 hours' rebuttable presumption).

2.3 Live Incident Response
2.3.1 Initial Stop / Encounter
Vehicle stop under s.108 RTA 1961, or MAT checkpoint stop under s.10 RTA 2010, or post-collision contact,
or observed driving with indicators.
Approach with safety - one Garda speaks; second observes.
Identification - driver's licence under s.39 RTA 1961.
Observation - smell of alcohol; bloodshot eyes; slurred speech; demeanour; the open container; the time of day;
the context.

2.3.2 Forming the Requirement to Test
Reasonable suspicion of consumption (s.10 RTA 2010); OR
MAT checkpoint random testing (s.10 with checkpoint authorisation); OR
Event-triggered requirement (after collision involving injury / damage - s.10 framework).

2.3.3 Preliminary Breath Test
Conduct the test using the approved device (Drager / Lion / current approved equipment). Procedure:
Identify yourself as a Garda.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

State the basis (reasonable suspicion / event / checkpoint).
Read the requirement clearly.
Explain consequence of refusal.
Administer test per device protocol.
Record result.

2.3.4 Result Outcomes
Negative result - if no other indicators of impairment, release; document outcome.
Positive result - arrest under s.4 / s.5 RTA 2010 (depending on circumstances); transport to station for evidential
procedure.
Refusal - arrest under s.14 RTA 2010 (refusal offence); document the refusal precisely.
Inability to provide (medical / equipment) - alternative procedures engage.

2.4 Track B - Station Procedure
(Treated in detail in Chapter 4. Outline:)
Custody process at station - member in charge.
Evidential breath test (s.12 RTA 2010) - approved device (Evidenzer).
OR blood / urine sample (s.13 RTA 2010) - taken by designated doctor, samples to MBRS.
Statutory waiting period typically observed.
Suspect right to second sample / division of sample.
Statutory form completion.
Charge sheet on result confirmed.

2.5 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
2.5.1 Specimen Arrest Words - s.4
'[Name], I am arresting you under section 4 of the Road Traffic Act 2010. I require you to provide a specimen
of breath / blood / urine. I have reasonable cause to suspect that you have committed an offence under section
4 of the Road Traffic Act 2010, namely driving a mechanically propelled vehicle in a public place while there
was present in your body a concentration of alcohol exceeding the specified limit. You are not obliged to say
anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in
evidence.'

2.5.2 Notebook Critical Entries
Time of observation / stop.
Basis for the stop (s.108 / MAT / collision / observation).
Vehicle details and driver identification.
Observations supporting reasonable suspicion (specific observations - smell, slur, conduct).
Preliminary breath test - time of requirement, result.
Arrest time and grounds.
Transport to station; time of arrival.
Each step in evidential procedure with timings.

2.6 Interview Plan
Drink-driving interview typically brief - the evidential case stands on the testing procedure. Cover:
Identification and confirmation of driving.
Account of alcohol consumption (timing, quantity, location) - the 'last drink' question is relevant to within-3hours analysis.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Any medical conditions affecting BAC reading.
Any medication that may affect.
Account of the journey.
Do not interview an intoxicated suspect to obtain admissions on driving - voluntariness considerations under
Madden. The evidential case rests on the testing procedure.

2.7 PULSE / File Index - Drink Driving
PULSE incident.
Notebook entries (member and any colleague).
BWV from stop / arrest.
Vehicle details and any related FCN.
Preliminary breath test result document.
Custody record.
Statutory form (evidential breath test or blood/urine).
MBRS certificate / Evidenzer report.
Calibration / approval documentation for testing equipment.
Charge sheet.
Prior antecedents (PULSE for prior drink-driving).

2.8 Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Driving / attempting to drive, intoxicant present over
limit

s.4 RTA 2010

In charge of vehicle (not driving) with intent to drive,
intoxicant present

s.5 RTA 2010

Refusal to provide preliminary breath sample

s.14 RTA 2010 refusal offence

Refusal to provide evidential sample at station

s.14 RTA 2010 refusal offence

Drink-driving causing serious harm / death

s.4 + s.53 RTA 1961 (dangerous driving causing serious
harm / death)

Drink-driving + driving without licence/insurance

s.4 + s.38/s.56

2.9 Specimen Wording - Statement of Member
'On [date] at [time] at [location], a public place, I observed [vehicle] being driven by [driver, identified by
licence at the scene]. The vehicle was being driven in a manner that suggested impairment - specifically
[observations]. I activated my BWV and signalled the vehicle to stop using emergency lighting. The vehicle
stopped at [location]. I approached the driver, identified myself, and detected a strong smell of alcohol on his
breath. His eyes were bloodshot and his speech was slurred. I formed reasonable cause to suspect that he had
consumed an intoxicant. At [time] I made a requirement under s.10 RTA 2010 for a preliminary breath test
using [device]. The result was positive. At [time] I arrested him under s.4 RTA 2010 and cautioned. He was
transported to [station] arriving at [time]. The custody process was managed by Member in Charge Garda
[name]. Evidential breath specimen was obtained at [time] on [device, calibration certificate available]. The
certified result was [reading]. The statutory form was completed at [time]. [Suspect] was charged with an
offence under s.4 RTA 2010 at [time].'

2.10 Defence Will Attack
Procedural compliance - testing procedure compliance (the Avadenei territory). Rebut by rigorous

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

documentation of each step and the statutory form completion.
Reasonable cause for preliminary test - the basis for the s.10 requirement challenged. Rebut by notebook capture
of specific observations.
Driving / attempting to drive - the defence may contend the accused was not driving (someone else; not
attempting). Rebut by direct observation; admissions; witness; CCTV.
Public place - the place was not within the s.3 definition. Rebut by location specifics.
Within 3 hours - timing challenge to the rebuttable presumption. Rebut by precise time records.
Medical conditions affecting reading - asthma, GERD, certain medications. Rebut by case-specific analysis
with MBRS / medical evidence.
Calibration / device approval - challenge to instrument certification. Rebut by certification documentation.
Identification of driver - rare but possible. Rebut by licence at scene + direct observation.

2.11 Red Flag Escalation
Drink-driving + serious / fatal collision - escalates to s.53 RTA 1961 + criminal investigation framework (Vol
7 Ch 5).
Drink-driving + serious injury short of fatal - s.53 may engage; consider s.3 / s.4 NFOAP (Vol 2) where
appropriate.
Drink-driving with extreme BAC (e.g., 3x limit) - aggravated sentencing context.
Multiple prior drink-driving convictions - high-end sentencing exposure.
Drink-driving with child passengers - aggravation.

2.12 Common Pitfalls
Procedural compliance shortfalls in testing procedure - the most common ground of acquittal.
Inadequate notebook capture of reasonable cause for preliminary test.
Failure to identify driver definitively (where multiple occupants of vehicle).
Statutory form omissions / errors.
Device calibration / approval evidence not available at trial.
Custody record gaps.

2.13 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 5 - integrity of testing procedure.
Standard 6 - impartial application of stop / test threshold.
Standard 4 - force in arrest proportionate (intoxicated suspects may be unsteady, distressed, unpredictable).
Welfare - custody-stage medical assessment where intoxication severe.

2.14 Compliment / Cross-References
Effective drink-driving case-build - procedurally rigorous, evidentially clean - is the operational standard.
Cross-reference: Ch.3 (drug-driving); Ch.4 (testing procedure in detail); Ch.5 (fatal collision where drink
involved); Case Law Appendix (Avadenei, Finn, Spillane procedural line).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - Drug Driving
Drug-driving is treated under the same s.4 RTA 2010 framework as drink-driving where impairment is the basis
of the offence. The 2014 / 2016 amendments introduced specific drug-driving thresholds for certain substances
(cannabis, cocaine, opioids) - the framework operates on threshold limits for these substances plus impairmentbased prosecution for other drug effects.

3.1 The Drug-Driving Framework
3.1.1 The Threshold Substances
The RTA 2010 framework (as amended) sets specific blood-concentration thresholds for certain controlled
drugs. Driving with concentration of these substances in blood above the specified limit is an offence regardless
of impairment proof:
Substance

Blood concentration limit

Cannabis (THC)

Specified threshold (low - effectively zero-tolerance for impaired use)

Cocaine and benzoylecgonine

Specified threshold

Heroin (6-MAM marker)

Specified threshold

Other specified substances

Per Regulations

(Specific concentrations set out in the Road Traffic Act 2010 (Section 4) (Specified Substances) Regulations check current values.)

3.1.2 Impairment-Based Offence
Where the substance is not within the specific-threshold framework (or in addition), the impairment limb
operates: driving while under the influence of an intoxicant (drug) to such extent as to be incapable of having
proper control of the vehicle. Field assessment indicators support prosecution:
Field impairment testing - approved tests (gaze nystagmus, walk and turn, one-leg stand, divided attention).
Garda observation - speech, coordination, demeanour.
Drug recognition by trained Garda.
Drug-driving roadside oral fluid test (where deployed).

3.2 Investigation Sequence
Stop / encounter as for drink-driving.
Observation - signs of drug impairment (pinpoint pupils suggest opioids; dilated pupils suggest stimulants;
cannabis odour; demeanour signs).
Preliminary drug test (oral fluid swab where deployed) OR impairment test OR forming suspicion based on
observations.
Arrest under s.4 RTA 2010 on drug-driving basis.
Transport to station.
Blood sample at station (s.13) - blood is the evidential sample for drug-driving; oral fluid is preliminary only.
MBRS analysis of blood.
Result determines threshold offence or impairment-based prosecution.

3.3 Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Threshold drug concentration exceeded in
blood

s.4 RTA 2010 (drug threshold)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Scenario

Charge

Impaired driving without specific threshold
breach

s.4 RTA 2010 (impairment)

Polydrug + alcohol

Multiple s.4 counts; or combined intoxicant impairment

Refusal of drug-driving test

s.14 RTA 2010 refusal

3.4 Common Pitfalls
Field impairment test conduct - must be per trained protocol.
Blood sample timing - degradation of drug indicators over time.
Drug recognition without specific training - prosecution rests on the evidence, not unsupported opinion.
Calibration / approval of preliminary drug test devices.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - Testing Procedure - s.9-15 RTA 2010
The testing procedure under the RTA 2010 framework is technically detailed and contested intensely at trial.
The Avadenei jurisprudence (2017) emphasises strict statutory compliance - shortfalls in procedure may render
the evidential reading inadmissible. This chapter sets out the procedure step-by-step.

4.1 The Procedural Architecture
s.9 RTA 2010 - definitions; equipment; designated doctor.
s.10 RTA 2010 - preliminary breath test power.
s.11 RTA 2010 - preliminary drug test.
s.12 RTA 2010 - station evidential breath specimen.
s.13 RTA 2010 - blood / urine evidential specimen.
s.14 RTA 2010 - refusal offences.
s.15 RTA 2010 - drug-driving testing specifics.

4.2 The Preliminary Breath Test - s.10
Requirement Bases
Reasonable suspicion of consumption.
MAT checkpoint (random).
After collision involving injury.
Where suspect is committing or has committed an RTA offence in member's view.

Procedure

4.3 The Station Evidential Procedure
4.3.1 Custody Stage
Member in Charge processes the arrest.
Suspect rights communicated.
Medical assessment where intoxication severe.
Solicitor consultation right (Gormley).

4.3.2 The Required Period
A statutory waiting period (typically 20 minutes) is observed between arrival at the station and the evidential
sample being taken. This period eliminates 'mouth alcohol' contamination of the reading. Document the period
precisely - the waiting starts on arrival; the sample is taken after the period elapses.

4.3.3 Statutory Requirement to Provide
The Garda makes a statutory requirement under s.12 RTA 2010 (evidential breath) OR s.13 RTA 2010 (blood
/ urine):
Read the requirement clearly.
Explain the choice (where applicable - between breath and blood/urine).
Explain the consequence of refusal (s.14 RTA 2010 offence - same penalty as the substantive offence).
Allow opportunity to consult solicitor.

4.3.4 Evidential Breath Sample - s.12

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Approved device (currently Evidenzer).
Two specimens are taken; the lower reading is used.
Device calibration certificate documented.
Statutory form completed - the form is the operative evidential document.
Suspect given a printout copy.
Reading converted to micrograms / 100ml breath.

4.3.5 Blood / Urine Sample - s.13
Designated doctor attends station (or nurse for blood).
Blood sample (preferred for drug-driving) OR urine sample.
Sample divided - one portion for prosecution; second portion offered to suspect.
Sealed in approved kit.
Transport to Medical Bureau of Road Safety (MBRS) Dublin.
MBRS analysis; certificate of analysis becomes evidence.
Statutory form completed.

4.4 The Avadenei Compliance Standard
DPP v Avadenei [2017] IESC 77 (Supreme Court) established that the statutory form completed during the
testing procedure is the evidential document - and that errors / omissions in the form may render the evidence
inadmissible. The form must be:
Completed accurately - all required fields entered.
Completed contemporaneously - at the time of the procedure.
Signed where required.
Copy provided to suspect.
Reflects the actual procedure conducted.
Operational implication: the Garda must complete the form with care; any deviation from the prescribed
procedure must be noted; the form is not a formality but the case foundation. See Case Law Appendix for full
vLex treatment of Avadenei.

4.5 Refusal Offences - s.14
Refusal or failure to provide a specimen (preliminary or evidential) is itself an offence under s.14 RTA 2010,
with the same penalty as the substantive drink/drug-driving offence. Operational considerations:
Refusal can be express ('I refuse') or implied (failure to comply when given opportunity).
The refusal must be 'without reasonable excuse' - the suspect has the evidential burden to put forward reasonable
excuse (medical, physical inability to provide).
Document precisely - what was said, what was done, the opportunity given.
Charge under s.14 rather than s.4 - though both may be charged in some cases.

4.6 The Designated Doctor Framework
Doctors on the panel under the RTA framework attend stations to take blood samples.
The doctor's certificate forms part of the evidence.
Doctor must give evidence at trial in contested cases.
Specific safeguards on sample taking, storage, transmission.

4.7 The Medical Bureau of Road Safety (MBRS)
Statutory body for analysis of evidential blood / urine samples (and historically alcohol).
Located at UCD.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operates the analysis chain from receipt of sample to certificate of analysis.
MBRS staff give evidence at trial when contested.

4.8 Common Procedural Pitfalls
20-minute waiting period not properly observed / documented.
Statutory form errors (Avadenei territory) - missing fields, signature gaps, incorrect entries.
Device calibration certificate not available.
Failure to offer / facilitate solicitor consultation (Gormley).
Refusal documentation incomplete.
Sample chain of custody gaps from station to MBRS.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 5 - Dangerous and Careless Driving
Quality-of-driving offences span from careless driving (a falling-below-standard offence with summary
disposal) through dangerous driving (more serious; either-way) to dangerous driving causing death or serious
bodily harm (indictable, up to 10 years). The framework is set out in ss.51-53 of the Road Traffic Act 1961.
This chapter treats each offence and the fatal collision investigation framework.

5.1 Careless Driving - s.52 RTA 1961
s.52 RTA 1961

Elements
Driving a vehicle in a public place: Standard driving + public place per RTA definition.
Without due care and attention: Falling below the standard of a competent and prudent driver. Objective
standard.
OR without reasonable consideration for others: Alternative limb - inconsiderate driving towards other road
users.

Court and Penalty
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: Fine; penalty points (typically 5 points).

Operational Examples
Failure to maintain proper lookout (rear-ending another vehicle in queue).
Inappropriate manoeuvre (sudden lane change without checking).
Speed inappropriate for conditions but not dangerous.
Failure to yield where required (without dangerous consequence).
Mobile phone-related lapses (where not specifically charged as mobile phone offence).

5.2 Dangerous Driving - s.51 RTA 1961
s.53 RTA 1961 (as amended) - paraphrased framework
[Note: Section numbering and exact text amended over time - verify current consolidated section number. The
substantive offence as set out above is the operational concept.]

Elements
Driving in a public place: Standard.
In a manner dangerous to the public: Objective standard - manner of driving (including speed) was dangerous
having regard to all circumstances. The danger need not be actualised - dangerous manner is the offence.

Court and Penalty
Court: Either-way; typically summary; indictable available.
Penalty: Summary: 6 months / fine / disqualification (2 years minimum). Indictment: up to 5 years.

Operational Examples
Excessive speed in built-up area.
Overtaking on bend / brow of hill.
Driving on wrong side of road.
Sustained reckless driving.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Driving while severely fatigued.
Pursued offender driving to escape.

5.3 Dangerous Driving Causing Death / Serious Bodily Harm - s.53 RTA 1961
s.53 RTA 1961

Elements
Driving in dangerous manner: As for s.51.
Causation: The dangerous driving caused the death / serious bodily harm. Standard causation analysis - factual
cause + legal cause; intervening events; foreseeability.
Death OR serious bodily harm: Either alternative.

Court and Penalty
Court: Indictable; Circuit Criminal Court.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment; substantial disqualification (typically minimum 2 years; can be lifetime
in serious cases).

5.4 The Quality-of-Driving Continuum
Standard

Offence

Mental State

Below reasonably prudent

s.52 careless

Negligence (objective)

Dangerous to public

s.51 dangerous

Objective danger

Dangerous
harm

s.53

Objective danger + causation

Recklessly endangering

s.13 NFOAP 1997 (Vol 2)

Subjective recklessness (Murray)

Murder by driving (extreme
cases)

Murder

Intent or s.4 Criminal Justice Act 1964
(intention to cause serious injury)

+

death/serious

5.5 Fatal Collision Investigation
Fatal collisions engage the full investigation framework. The criminal investigation (typically under s.53 RTA
1961 or other charge) operates alongside the Coroner's investigation and the family liaison function. This
section sets out the operational framework.

5.5.1 First Response at Scene
5.5.2 The Forensic Collision Investigator (FCI)
Specialist Garda resource - typically Divisional or shared regional.
Scene examination - vehicle dynamics, road conditions, marks, debris analysis.
Mathematical reconstruction - speed, trajectory, point of impact.
FCI report becomes principal evidence in serious collision cases.
Standard members support FCI through scene preservation.

5.5.3 Family Liaison
Family Liaison Officer (FLO) assigned - typically trained Garda member.
Death notification handled with care - in person, with appropriate support.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Sustained engagement - through investigation, prosecution, Coroner's inquest.
Information sharing within proper limits.
Welfare support for family throughout.

5.5.4 The Coroner Interface
Sudden / unexpected death - mandatory Coroner notification.
Coroner directs post-mortem.
Coroner's inquest follows criminal proceedings (if any).
Garda gives evidence at inquest.
Inquest determines who, when, where, how (manner of death).

5.5.5 The Investigation File
Fatal collision files are major. The structure:

5.6 Sentencing in Quality-of-Driving Offences
Aggravating factors in s.51 / s.52 / s.53 sentencing:
Speed substantially above limit.
Alcohol / drug impairment (concurrent s.4 charge or aggravation).
Sustained reckless / dangerous pattern.
Multiple victims.
Pedestrian / vulnerable road user victim.
Mobile phone / distraction-related.
Disqualified driver / no insurance.
Prior similar offences.
Failure to stop / report.
Mitigators: genuine remorse; plea; absence of intoxicant; lack of prior; circumstances of driving (emergency
etc.).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 6 - Other Road Traffic Offences
6.1 Driving Without Licence - s.38 RTA 1961
s.38 RTA 1961

Operational Application
Encountered in routine stops and at MAT checkpoints.
s.39 RTA 1961 - power to demand production of licence at the scene.
Driver without licence at scene - given period to produce at station (typically 10 days).
Failure to produce within period - offence.
Distinction: never held licence vs licence expired vs disqualified vs prohibited (banned).

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Driving without ever having held licence

s.38 RTA 1961

Driving with expired licence

s.38 RTA 1961

Driving while disqualified

s.38 + RTA 2002 disqualification provisions (aggravated)

Failure to produce licence after demand

s.40 RTA 1961

Penalty
Fine; disqualification; penalty points; up to 6 months imprisonment for driving while disqualified (substantially
aggravated).

6.2 Driving Without Insurance - s.56 RTA 1961
s.56 RTA 1961

Operational Application
One of the most serious 'routine' offences - the social cost of uninsured driving is substantial.
Insurance certificate / disc check at the scene.
Verification via Garda PEMS / Motor Insurers Bureau database.
Distinction: uninsured (no policy); driver not covered under existing policy (exclusions); fraudulent policy.

Penalty
Fine; disqualification (typically 2 years minimum on first offence); 5 penalty points; up to 6 months
imprisonment in serious cases.

6.3 Failure to Stop / Report (Hit and Run) - s.106 RTA 1961
s.106 RTA 1961

Operational Application
Common in collision contexts where one party leaves the scene.
Hit and run on pedestrian - serious; engages s.53 / NFOAP framework if injury.
Parked-vehicle damage - the s.106 obligation applies.
Investigation typically retrospective - identifying driver / vehicle from witness, CCTV, debris.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Suspect identification - vehicle damage matched to scene debris; paint transfer; license plate.

Penalty
Substantial - reflecting the offence against road safety and the assistance owed to victims. Disqualification
routine.

6.4 Distraction and Protection Offences
6.4.1 Mobile Phone Use - s.3 RTA 2006
Using a mobile phone while driving (other than hands-free in certain contexts) is an offence. FCN typical; 3
penalty points; fine. Aggravating context where dangerous driving / collision follows.

6.4.2 Seatbelt - RTA 2006 / Regulations
Drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts. Driver responsible for under-17 passengers. FCN typical; 3 penalty
points; fine.

6.4.3 Helmet - Motorcycle
Motorcyclist (driver and passenger) must wear helmet. FCN; penalty points; fine.

6.5 Scrambler / Off-Road Vehicle Offences
Recent legislation (Road Traffic and Roads Act 2023 framework) addressed the public-nuisance use of
scramblers, quads, and off-road motorcycles in urban public spaces. Operational features:
Specific offences of using scramblers / off-road vehicles in public places where insurance / licence requirements
not met.
Power to seize scramblers used in offences.
Operational coordination with local communities affected by scrambler antisocial use.
Charge selection may include scrambler-specific offence + s.38 / s.56 / s.51 / s.13 NFOAP endangerment.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 7 - Road Traffic Powers
This chapter consolidates the principal Garda powers in road traffic enforcement: stop, checkpoint, production,
testing, arrest.

7.1 Stop Power - s.108 RTA 1961
s.108 RTA 1961

Application
General stop power - no specific cause required to stop a vehicle for routine check.
In practice, stops are typically for: traffic enforcement; suspicious indicators; MAT operation; checkpoint
operation; observed offence.
The lawful basis of the stop is operationally important for any subsequent investigation.
Stop conduct - hi-vis; safe location; clear signal; identify yourself; explain reason for stop.

7.2 Mandatory Alcohol Testing (MAT) Checkpoint - s.10 RTA 2010
Authorisation
Inspector-level authorisation required for MAT operation.
Authorisation specifies time, place, and basis.
Authorisation document carried by checkpoint commander.
Pattern of stopping (e.g., every Nth vehicle) specified.

Operation
Hi-vis identification of operation.
Safe positioning.
Selection of vehicles per authorisation pattern.
Each driver: preliminary breath test under s.10 RTA 2010.
Driver who fails / refuses - arrest and station procedure.
Driver who passes - released.
Records: vehicles stopped, results, outcomes.

Common Operational Issues
Authorisation expiry - the operation must be within the authorised period.
Pattern deviation - stops must follow the authorised pattern; targeted individual stops outside the pattern are
not MAT-grounded.
Documentation - the checkpoint log is part of the case file.

7.3 Production of Driving Licence - s.39 RTA 1961
Power to require production of driving licence:
Demand at the scene - driver produces at the scene.
Where driver does not have at scene - given period (typically 10 days) to produce at nominated station.
Failure to produce within period - s.40 offence.

7.4 Testing Powers - s.10-15 RTA 2010
(Treated in detail in Chapter 4. Summary here.)

7.5 Arrest Powers in Road Traffic
s.39 RTA 2002 - Drink Driving Arrest
Power to arrest without warrant for drink-driving offence based on positive preliminary breath test or other
grounds of reasonable suspicion of s.4 / s.5 RTA 2010 offence.

Standard Arrest Powers
Arrest under Walsh reasonable suspicion for any arrestable RTA offence (dangerous driving causing death,
etc.). s.4 CJA 1984 detention regime engages where indictable offence.

Arrest for Refusal - s.14
Where driver refuses to provide preliminary or evidential specimen, arrest under s.14 RTA 2010.

7.6 Vehicle Seizure Powers
Where vehicle used in offence (no insurance / no licence) - seizure power under specific RTA framework.
Scrambler / off-road vehicle seizure under recent legislation.
Forensic preservation seizure in collision investigation.
Where vehicle is evidence - exhibits framework.

7.7 Pursuit Framework
Pursuit of fleeing vehicle is one of the higher-risk operational decisions. Standard framework:
Proportionality - the risk of pursuit weighed against the offence and the risk of allowing escape.
Authorisation - command involvement at certain stages.
Risk assessment - speed, conditions, location, suspect behaviour.
Communication - constant radio contact with control.
Decision to discontinue - active decision where risk profile shifts.
Documentation - the pursuit log and post-pursuit report.
(See Garda Pursuit Policy for current framework.)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Defences, Burdens, Charge Selection, File
Craft (Road Traffic)
Part A - Burdens
Standard criminal burdens. Specific RTA features:
Within-3-hours presumption for BAC reading - rebuttable; suspect has evidential burden to put rebuttal in issue.
Reasonable excuse for refusal - suspect evidential burden.
Specific statutory presumptions on driving, ownership, intoxicant identification - check specific provisions.

Part B - RTA-Specific Defences
B.1 Procedural Challenge (the Avadenei Path)
Most contested route. Errors in statutory form; deviation from prescribed procedure; calibration gaps; specimen
chain issues. Rebut by procedural rigour and complete documentation.

B.2 Reasonable Cause Challenge
The basis for stop / preliminary test challenged. Rebut by notebook capture of specific articulable observations.

B.3 Not Driving / Not Attempting
In drink-driving / quality-of-driving cases - the suspect contends he was not driving (asleep in vehicle / not the
driver / not attempting). Rebut by direct observation, witness, CCTV, vehicle context (engine running, lights
on, key position).

B.4 Not Public Place
The location not within the s.3 RTA definition of public place. Rebut by location-specific evidence on access
by public.

B.5 Medical / Mechanical
Medical conditions affecting BAC reading (GERD, asthma, certain medications).
Mechanical defect not driver's fault (dangerous driving).
Emergency / necessity defence (rare).

B.6 Mistaken Identity (Hit and Run)
Driver identification challenged. Rebut by forensic links (debris, paint, witness ID, CCTV).

B.7 Causation (s.53)
In dangerous driving causing death - the defence may contend the driving did not cause the death (intervening
conduct, pre-existing medical condition). Rebut by FCI reconstruction and pathology.

Appendix - Evidence and Admissibility (Road Traffic)
Part A - The Statutory Form
The s.12 / s.13 RTA 2010 statutory form is the operative evidential document for drink/drug-driving cases. Per
Avadenei, strict compliance is required:
Every required field completed.
Accurate entries reflecting actual procedure.
Contemporaneous completion.
Signatures where required.
Copy provided to suspect.
Bilingual completion where applicable.

Part B - Calibration and Approval
Approved devices only - statutory approval framework.
Calibration certificate at time of use - documented.
Specific calibration history retained.
Operator training certified.

Part C - Chain of Custody - Blood / Urine
From station collection through to MBRS analysis:
Designated doctor takes sample under prescribed conditions.
Sample divided - prosecution portion + suspect portion.
Sealed in approved kit.
Signed for at each transfer.
Transit to MBRS Dublin.
MBRS receipt and storage.
Analysis with certificate.
Certificate evidence at trial.

Part D - The MBRS Certificate
Issued by Medical Bureau of Road Safety.
Specifies the substance and concentration.
Admissible as evidence of the analysis.
MBRS staff give evidence at trial in contested cases.

Part E - Field Impairment Testing (Drug-Driving)
Trained Gardai conduct field tests.
Approved test protocols (gaze nystagmus, walk and turn, one-leg stand, divided attention).
Test results documented contemporaneously.
Member-trained-and-certified for test conduct.

Part F - FCI Reports (Fatal Collision)
Forensic Collision Investigator report admitted as expert evidence.
Includes scene examination, vehicle dynamics analysis, reconstruction.

FCI gives evidence at trial.
Supports causation analysis in s.53 cases.

Part G - BWV in Road Traffic
BWV activation at the stop / arrest is operationally standard.
Capture of: reasonable cause observations; the stop; the testing requirement; the result; the arrest.
BWV admissible as evidence.

Part H - CCTV in Road Traffic
Traffic cameras - red light, speed.
Local authority CCTV.
Private business CCTV (forecourts, shops).
Vehicle dashcams - private and bus / taxi.
Evidence recovery and chain of custody.

Appendix - Fatal Collision Investigation Framework
Fatal collisions engage the full investigation framework alongside the Coroner's investigation and family liaison
function. This appendix consolidates the operational standard.

Part A - First Response Protocol

Part B - The FCI Engagement
Call-out at the earliest opportunity.
FCI scene examination - vehicle dynamics, road conditions, marks.
Mathematical reconstruction - speed, trajectory, point of impact.
FCI report - principal evidence in serious collision cases.

Part C - Pathology and Toxicology
Coroner directs post-mortem.
Pathology - cause of death, injuries pattern.
Toxicology of deceased - alcohol, drugs.
Driver toxicology if surviving.

Part D - Family Liaison Officer Function
FLO assigned at the earliest stage.
Death notification with care.
Sustained engagement through investigation.
Coroner's inquest preparation and accompaniment.
Welfare support.

Part E - The Coroner Interface
Mandatory notification of sudden / unexpected death.
Coroner directs post-mortem.
Inquest follows criminal proceedings.
Garda evidence at inquest.
Verdict on manner of death.

Part F - Charge Analysis
s.53 RTA 1961 (dangerous driving causing death) - principal charge in many cases.
Manslaughter (where dangerous conduct beyond s.53 - extreme cases).
Drink/drug-driving (s.4) charged with s.53 where intoxicant involved.
Failure to stop / report (s.106) where applicable.
No insurance / licence / disqualified - aggravating.

Appendix - Statutory Anchors (Vol 7 - Road Traffic)

Part A - Drink / Drug Driving (RTA 2010)
A.1 s.4 RTA 2010 - Driving while Intoxicated
Driving / attempting to drive a mechanically propelled vehicle in a public place with concentration of alcohol /
specified intoxicant in blood / urine / breath exceeding specified limit. Penalty - up to 6 months / fine /
disqualification. Indictable in some scenarios.

A.2 s.5 RTA 2010 - Being in Charge while Intoxicated
Being in charge of mechanically propelled vehicle in public place with intent to drive, intoxicant present
exceeding limit. Penalty - fine / disqualification.

A.3 s.9-15 RTA 2010 - Testing Procedure
s.9 - definitions; equipment; designated doctor.
s.10 - preliminary breath test.
s.11 - preliminary drug test.
s.12 - station evidential breath.
s.13 - blood / urine.
s.14 - refusal offences.
s.15 - drug-driving specifics.

Part B - Quality of Driving (RTA 1961)
B.1 s.51 RTA 1961 - Dangerous Driving
Driving in manner (including speed) dangerous to public having regard to circumstances. Summary or
indictable.

B.2 s.52 RTA 1961 - Careless Driving
Driving without due care and attention or without reasonable consideration. Summary.

B.3 s.53 RTA 1961 - Dangerous Driving Causing Death / Serious Harm
Dangerous driving causing death / serious bodily harm. Up to 10 years on indictment.

Part C - Document / Standard Offences
C.1 s.38 RTA 1961 - No Licence
Driving in public place without holding driving licence. Aggravated where driving while disqualified.

C.2 s.56 RTA 1961 - No Insurance
Using mechanically propelled vehicle in public place without effective insurance / exempt status.

C.3 s.106 RTA 1961 - Failure to Stop / Report
Driver involved in collision causing injury / damage shall stop, remain, provide information, report to Garda.

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 7 - Road Traffic)
Road traffic case law engages a distinct procedural-rigour jurisprudence (Avadenei and the s.12 RTA 2010
statutory form line; Finn and calibration; Spillane and specimen chain), the dangerous-driving / causing-death
authorities, and the Volume 2 procedural authorities applied to road traffic. This appendix treats each in vLexstyle depth.
Source verification reminder
Road traffic case law continues to develop, particularly on procedural compliance with the statutory testing
framework. Verify all citations on vLex Ireland, BAILII, or the Courts Service judgments database before relying.
The Avadenei jurisprudence is the operational touchstone for the testing procedure.

Part A - The Avadenei Statutory Form Line
A.1 DPP v Avadenei [2017] IESC 77 - LANDMARK
Citation: [2017] IESC 77 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Avadenei was prosecuted for drink-driving. The statutory form completed during the testing procedure
at the station contained an error in its English-language declarations (the form was bilingual; the Englishlanguage section had a misstatement of the procedure). The defence challenged the admissibility of the breath
alcohol reading on grounds that the statutory form did not properly reflect the prescribed procedure.
Holding: The Supreme Court held that strict compliance with the statutory form is required. Errors / omissions
in the form may render the breath alcohol evidence inadmissible. The court applied the wider statutory
framework purposively but emphasised that the form is the operative evidential document and its accuracy is
constitutive of the prosecution case. Avadenei's conviction was overturned.
Reasoning: The court analysed the statutory framework under the RTA 2010 / earlier legislation. The form
serves multiple functions: documenting the procedure; informing the suspect of his rights and the offence;
recording the evidential reading. Errors in the form undermine each of these functions and the evidential basis
of the prosecution. The court rejected a 'substantial compliance' approach in favour of strict compliance.
Significance: Landmark for drink/drug-driving prosecutions. The statutory form is the operative evidential
document and its accuracy is required for admissibility. Operational consequence:
Every field must be completed accurately.
Contemporaneous completion - at the time of the procedure.
Signed where required.
Suspect provided with copy.
Reflects the actual procedure conducted.
Operational Application: The form is not a formality. Garda training on form completion is operationally
critical. Errors / deviations from the prescribed procedure must be noted (a true record is better than a fabricated
'clean' record - the latter is fatal to integrity). Where the procedure has been compromised, the case may not
succeed on the testing evidence - alternative basis (refusal under s.14, impairment evidence) may be considered.

A.2 DPP v Finn (Court of Criminal Appeal authority)
Significance: Authority on calibration / approval of breath testing devices. The device must be properly
calibrated and approved at the time of use. Calibration certificate becomes evidence. Operational application:
calibration documentation is part of the file; gaps in calibration evidence undermine the case.

A.3 DPP v Spillane (Court of Criminal Appeal authority)
Significance: Chain of custody of blood / urine specimens from station through to MBRS analysis. Each step
in the chain must be documented. Gaps undermine admissibility. Operational application: the specimen kit,
transport, MBRS receipt, analysis chain are all part of the file.

A.4 DPP v McGarrigle

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Significance: Evidence of consumption in drink-driving cases. The prosecution case is built on the testing
evidence; the suspect's statements on consumption may be relevant but are not constitutive. Operational
application: prioritise testing evidence; the case stands on the procedure compliance, not on suspect admissions.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part B - Dangerous and Careless Driving Jurisprudence
B.1 DPP v Cabot - Careless Threshold
Significance: Authority on the careless / dangerous driving threshold. Careless driving is below the standard
of a competent and prudent driver; dangerous driving requires that the manner of driving be dangerous to the
public. The distinction matters for charge selection.
Operational Application: Charge proportionate to conduct. Where the conduct is at the borderline, consider
the full picture - speed, location, road conditions, traffic density, presence of vulnerable road users - and select
charge accordingly.

B.2 DPP v Gillane - Dangerous Driving Causing Death
Significance: Court of Appeal authority on s.53 RTA 1961. Causation analysis - the dangerous driving caused
the death. Standard causation framework applies (factual cause + legal cause; intervening events;
foreseeability).
Operational Application: Build the causation evidence in fatal collision investigation - FCI reconstruction,
scene analysis, vehicle dynamics, witness accounts.

B.3 DPP v O'Brien and Other Authorities
Significance: Various authorities on RTA prosecutions across the spectrum. Specific procedural and
substantive points refined through case law.

B.4 Sentencing in Dangerous Driving Causing Death
Court of Appeal has developed sentencing guidance for s.53 cases. Aggravating factors: alcohol/drugs
(cumulative with s.4 RTA 2010); sustained dangerous driving; multiple victims; vulnerable victim (pedestrian,
cyclist, child); mobile phone / distraction; disqualified driver; failure to stop; prior similar offences. Mitigators:
genuine remorse; plea; circumstances of driving; absence of intoxicant; first offence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part C - Procedural Authorities Applied to Road Traffic
C.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Arrest Reasonable Suspicion
Operational Application (RTA): RTA arrests under s.39 RTA 2002 / s.14 / other framework require the
arresting Garda's reasonable suspicion of the offence. Notebook capture of the basis.

C.2 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness
Operational Application (RTA): Drink/drug-driving suspects are often intoxicated. Interview voluntariness
considerations engage. The case typically does not rest on interview admissions - the testing procedure provides
the evidence.

C.3 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 - Inferences
Operational Application (RTA): Inferences typically less significant in RTA cases - the case rests on the
testing procedure. Inferences may engage in dangerous-driving / hit-and-run / collision-investigation contexts.

C.4 DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 - Pre-Interview Solicitor
Operational Application (RTA): Drink-driving suspects at station have pre-interview consultation rights.
Facilitate at custody stage. The right operates even where the principal evidence is the breath test rather than
interview.

C.5 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 - Modern Exclusionary Rule
Operational Application (RTA): RTA cases are intensely contested on procedural grounds. JC margin is
narrow. Procedural rigour at every step - the Avadenei strict-compliance standard intersects with the JC
framework.

C.6 The People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - Identification
Operational Application (RTA): Identification engages in hit-and-run cases where the driver is identified
after the event. VIPER, ADVOKATE assessment supports contested cases.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part D - Constitutional and Convention Dimensions
D.1 Article 6 ECHR / Article 38.1 Constitution - Fair Trial
Testing-procedure compliance is part of the fair-trial guarantee. Procedural shortcuts that affect the suspect's
ability to challenge the evidence engage Article 6. The Avadenei framework operates within this.

D.2 Article 38.1 / Self-Incrimination
The statutory requirement to provide a sample (breath, blood, urine) has been the subject of constitutional
challenge. The Irish courts have upheld the framework on the basis that the regulatory road traffic context
justifies the requirement; the European Court of Human Rights has reached similar conclusions on equivalent
regimes.

D.3 Article 8 ECHR - Privacy
Blood / urine sampling engages bodily integrity. The framework operates within proportionate restrictions on
Article 8 - prescribed by law, pursuing legitimate aim of road safety, proportionate.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part E - Sentencing in Road Traffic
E.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250
Operational Application (RTA): Tiernan structured discretion applies to all RTA sentencing. Specific RTA
aggravators / mitigators within the framework.

E.2 Disqualification - The Mandatory Frameworks
RTA framework includes mandatory minimum disqualification periods for certain offences:
Drink-driving (s.4 RTA 2010) - minimum 6 months for first offence; longer for higher BAC and subsequent
offences.
Drug-driving - similar framework.
Dangerous driving (s.51) - minimum 2 years.
Dangerous driving causing death (s.53) - typically substantial disqualification; can be lifetime.
No insurance (s.56) - 2 years typical first offence.

E.3 Penalty Points Disqualification
Separate from offence-specific disqualification, accumulation of 12 penalty points within 3-year window results
in 6-month disqualification under the RTA 2002 PP system. Learner drivers - 7-point threshold.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part F - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
F.1 The Avadenei Imperative
The statutory form is the case foundation. Strict compliance. Accurate. Contemporaneous. Signed where
required. Copy to suspect. Reflects the actual procedure.

F.2 Calibration and Chain
The testing device must be properly calibrated and approved. The certification documentation is part of the file.
The blood / urine specimen chain from station to MBRS must be documented at every step.

F.3 Reasonable Suspicion for Stops / Tests
Where the procedure depends on reasonable suspicion (s.10 reasonable suspicion of consumption), the basis
must be articulable and contemporaneously documented. Generic descriptions do not suffice.

F.4 The MAT Framework Is Powerful but Disciplined
MAT operates without individual reasonable suspicion - but only where the operation is properly authorised,
the pattern of stops is followed, and the framework is observed. Deviation undermines the case.

F.5 Fatal Collision Investigation
FCI engagement, scene preservation, evidence handling, witness identification - the file build supports the
criminal investigation, the Coroner's inquest, and the family's understanding.

F.6 Disqualification Framework
Disqualification is part of the RTA sentencing architecture. The Penalty Points system operates alongside.
Cumulative disqualification framework where multiple offences.

INVESTIGATION

Volume 8 - Special Incidents
Operational Reference for An Garda Siochana
DMR North Central - Mountjoy Garda Station

For probationer and experienced member reference

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 8 - A Note on the Material
This volume covers incidents that engage the Garda role outside the standard criminal investigation framework:
sudden and unexpected death; missing persons; mental health detention; family liaison; and other special
incidents. The work is often emotionally demanding and procedurally distinctive. The Garda member is
sometimes the most useful person at the worst moment in someone's life - that responsibility shapes how the
work is done.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use Volume 8
Volume 8 covers special incidents - those operational categories that engage the Garda response across
criminal-investigative, welfare, public-safety, and family-support dimensions:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Special Incidents Operational Framework
Special incidents are united by a common operational reality: the Garda response is shaped not only by the
criminal-investigation framework but by:
The welfare dimension - protecting the living and dignifying the deceased.
The family dimension - sustained engagement, sometimes for years.
The multi-agency dimension - Coroner, hospital, mental health services, social services, NGOs.
The State-obligation dimension - Article 2 ECHR procedural obligations in deaths; Article 5 / 8 in mental health
detention.
The professional integrity dimension - the work is closely scrutinised and remembered by families for the rest
of their lives.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 8 Core Mnemonics
DIGNIFIED - Sudden Death First Response
D - Determine death (or commence resuscitation if life signs).
I - Identify deceased where possible (without prejudice to family notification).
G - Garda assessment - signs of suspicious circumstance, third party, scene preservation indicators.
N - Notify Sergeant; control room; Coroner liaison.
I - Initial scene preservation pending classification.
F - Family - identification, contact, notification protocol.
I - Investigation steps (witness statements; scene photography; relevant exhibits).
E - Engage Coroner liaison for post-mortem direction.
D - Document everything contemporaneously.

LOCATE - Missing Persons Investigation
L - Listen to the reporter (account, vulnerability, history).
O - Open PULSE incident immediately.
C - Categorise risk (high / medium / low / no apparent risk).
A - Action high-risk - DSCB Missing Persons Bureau, search, public appeals.
T - Track investigation steps (places searched, persons spoken to, communications checked).
E - Engage family and support.

CALM - Mental Health Encounter
C - Communicate calmly; reduce stimulation.
A - Assess - is the person at risk of self-harm or harm to others; can they be safely supported.
L - Liaise - HSE mental health services; GP; family; approved centre.
M - Move under s.12 MHA 2001 power if criteria met and proportionate.

FLO - Family Liaison Officer Function
F - First contact - the death notification or initial engagement.
L - Liaison sustained - throughout investigation and beyond.
O - Outreach - linking family to support services and information.
Verification reminder
Coroners legislation has been substantially modernised by the Coroners (Amendment) Act 2019. Mental Health
Act 2001 has been amended; review process and rights frameworks have developed. Missing persons protocols
are subject to ongoing operational policy at Garda HQ level. Verify current statutory text and operational policy
before relying.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Special Incidents Application
Special incidents engage the Code of Ethics in operationally distinctive ways. The dignity of the deceased; the
welfare of the bereaved; the proportionality of intervention with persons in mental health crisis; the integrity of
long-running missing persons investigations - all engage Standards heightened by the human stakes involved.

Standard 1 - Duty
The duty to protect life manifests in special incidents as: effective response to mental health crisis (preventing
suicide / self-harm); proactive missing-persons investigation (locating before harm occurs); thorough suddendeath investigation (engaging Article 2 procedural obligations).

Standard 3 - Diligence
Special incidents require sustained engagement over time. Missing persons cases may extend across years.
Sudden death investigations engage Coroner's inquest months or years after. Family liaison continues through
court proceedings and beyond. Diligence over time is the operational requirement.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Mental health detentions occasionally require force - persons in acute crisis may resist. Force must be lawful,
necessary, proportionate. The s.12 MHA framework is welfare not punitive - the encounter is fundamentally
different from criminal arrest. Specialist resources (Critical Incident Team where available) support high-risk
situations.

Standard 5 - Honesty and Integrity
Sudden death and missing persons families remember every interaction. Honesty in what is communicated,
when, and how - including honesty about what is not known - builds trust. Speculation, partial accounts, or
evasion damage trust irretrievably.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
Vulnerable persons across the demographic spectrum engage the special incidents framework. Mental health
detentions, missing persons investigations, sudden death responses must be conducted to the same standard
regardless of the person's circumstances. Targeted handling based on improper factors (homelessness,
addiction, immigration status) is a Standard 6 trip-wire.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Special incidents work occasionally encounters systemic gaps - mental health services unavailable; child
protection responses inadequate; missing persons supports underdeveloped. The duty to speak up applies through internal channels, through formal complaints / reviews where systemic failures contribute to harm.

Six Special Incidents Trip-Wires

The Welfare Dimension - Investigators
Special incidents work carries cumulative occupational psychological cost - sustained exposure to death,
distress, family grief, sometimes traumatic scenes. Garda welfare services and peer support frameworks
operate. Critical Incident Stress Debriefing where applicable. Welfare engagement is professional, not
